<!-- GENERATED by cc-guides from .claude/fragments/AGENTS.md/ ‚Äî do not edit; edit the fragments and push ‚Äî CI renders. -->
# fusekit Development Guide

Revisioned catalog, tenant convergence, native mount, and File Provider runtime.

## Repository structure

```text
fusekit/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ catalog/            # SQLite WAL object identity, namespace, snapshots, changes, state
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ catalogworker/      # generation-fenced catalog child and exact proxy
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tenant/             # generation-fenced actors and PrepareTenant convergence
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sourceauthority/    # recursive observation, snapshot repair, mutation, source barriers
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sourcedriver/       # semantic source contract, target sets, mutation receipts
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sourcedriverproto/  # generated exact v1 source-driver schema
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sourcedriverservice/ # exact persistent-session source-driver protocol
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sourcedriverruntime/ # authoritative snapshot/delta convergence and recovery
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ contentstream/      # immutable bounded content streaming
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mountmux/           # one native root, route pins, CatalogFS, signed child callbacks
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ holder/             # composed daemonkit process runtime
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ catalogservice/     # exact persistent-session catalog protocol
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mountservice/       # exact persistent-session tenant/native protocol
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ catalogproto/       # generated exact v1 catalog schema
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mountproto/         # generated exact v1 mount schema
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ transportproto/     # generated v1 suite identity and schema fingerprints
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ convergence/        # demand-aware targeting, coalescing, acknowledgements, quarantine
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Sources/FuseKit/    # Swift File Provider runtime, domain controller, signed broker
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ fuset/              # versioned FUSE-T packaging input and install facts
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ docs/reports/       # rewrite evidence ledger
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ .github/workflows/  # Go, fuse-tag, Swift, schema, guide, and release gates
```

The root package exports stable tenant aliases only. There is no standalone
holder application: every consumer embeds `holder.Runtime` in its own fixed
signed app. `holder.NewRuntimePlan` produces the exact `RuntimePlan` and
daemon-facing `DeploymentPlan`; the runtime role calls `holder.RunChild` before
normal startup.

The v1.6 line is a hard cut. The pre-v1.6 in-process mount and raw mount-request
surfaces, `mountd`, `mountset`, legacy holder and live probes, content
bridge/tree APIs, `holderfs`, overlay selection, old File Provider control,
per-directory lease/strike state, retirement journals/breakers, and
compatibility wire surfaces do not return. The current `tenant.MountSpec` is
only immutable presentation metadata within a `TenantSpec`.

## Testing

Run `scripts/test.sh`, never bare `go test` on a real machine. The standard gate
is:

```sh
scripts/test.sh -race -count=1 ./...
go vet ./...
CGO_ENABLED=0 go build ./...
go run ./catalogproto/gen -check
go run ./catalogworker/gen -check
go run ./mountproto/gen -check
go run ./sourcedriverproto/gen -check
go run ./transportproto/gen -check
swift build
swift test
```

Fuse-tag compilation is safe with its provider installed; live mount,
process-kill, File Provider, and TCC exercises are VM-only.

## Ask Before Assuming

When the user's request has ambiguity ‚Äî unclear scope, multiple plausible interpretations, undefined edge cases, or unspecified tradeoffs ‚Äî stop and ask. Propose 2-4 concrete options and let the user pick, or list the assumptions you'd otherwise make and ask which ones hold. There is no such thing as too many questions; one wrong implementation costs more than ten clarifying exchanges. Default to interrogating the user when in doubt ‚Äî multiple short questions early beat a wrong direction later.

## Code Review Response (Plan Re-Entry)

When the user reviews code you wrote and re-enters plan mode ‚Äî whether by leaving inline diff comments, pasting a numbered list of issues, or otherwise sending review-shaped feedback after a recent edit cycle ‚Äî you MUST:

0. **Delegate context-gathering to a subagent.** Spawn one `Explore` subagent with every cite (file:line + the user's verbatim comment text). Instruct it to, per cite, `Grep` the file with ~5 lines of context either side of the cited line (`-B 5 -A 5`), and only escalate to a full `Read` when the ¬±5-line window is insufficient (e.g. the comment refers to a function defined further up). Have it also surface sibling call sites with the same issue (Grep across the module). Use the subagent's digest as your source of truth when drafting the plan. Do NOT bulk-`Read` the cited files yourself in the main turn ‚Äî it bloats the main context window before you've even started writing the plan.
1. **Draft a new plan**, not a code change. Plan-mode re-entry is the user asking "let's align on what you'll do next," not "go fix it."
2. **Inline every comment verbatim** in the plan. Each comment gets a short anchor (`#N`, the file:line if provided, or a quoted excerpt) plus the user's exact wording in a blockquote or `*"‚Ä¶"*` italics. Do not paraphrase. The user must be able to scan the plan and see every comment they wrote reproduced exactly.
3. **Cluster when many.** If there are more than ~5 comments, group them into themes (e.g. "T1 ‚Äî Guards against impossible states") and list every verbatim trigger per theme. Address every cited line *and* extrapolate the rule to other call sites that have the same problem.
4. **Map every comment.** Maintain a "verbatim feedback table" near the end of the plan with one row per comment: `# | file:line | verbatim | cluster`. No comment may be silently dropped.
5. **Do NOT start implementing** before the plan is approved via `ExitPlanMode`. Delegating reads via #0 is fine; editing source is not.

The canonical shape is the `Overarching themes` table + per-cluster `**#N (verbatim):** *"‚Ä¶"*` anchors + final mapping table. When a comment is ambiguous, ask via `AskUserQuestion` rather than guessing.

### Plan follow-up questions

After you write a plan, the user may respond with questions ("why this approach?", "what about X?", "did you consider Y?") rather than approval. In that case you MUST NOT edit the plan to bake in answers. Instead:

1. **Answer the question conversationally** in your text response ‚Äî explain the reasoning, the tradeoffs, and what you'd recommend.
2. **Propose options via `AskUserQuestion`** ‚Äî one question per ambiguity, each with 2‚Äì4 concrete options the user can pick from. Batch related questions into one `AskUserQuestion` call.
3. **Wait for the user's choice** before editing the plan. The plan edit then reflects the user's pick, not your assumption.

Editing the plan first robs the user of the choice and forces them to diff the plan to find what you decided. Surface the decision point first.

## Parallelize Independent Work

Sequential is the exception, not the default, and saturation is a hard requirement, not a preference: at every dispatch point, every lane whose inputs are ready is in flight ‚Äî one runnable lane dispatched while another runnable lane waits is a scheduling bug. Two steps that don't consume each other's output run at the same time; when unsure whether they're independent, assume they are and fan out. Even verdict-gated work overlaps: whenever a gate is pending ‚Äî a verification verdict, a CI run, a review pass ‚Äî and a concrete candidate for the gated next step exists, the candidate gets built, worktree-isolated, while the gate resolves (CLAUDE.md ¬ß Plan Execution & Orchestration, Speculate across every gate). The orchestrator routes and synthesizes ‚Äî it never executes work a subagent could, including sustained browser automation, QA sweeps, and data extraction. Pick the surface by scale:

- **Batch tool calls in one message** ‚Äî the cheapest parallelism and the most missed. Independent reads, greps, globs, and read-only Bash go in a *single* message, never one per turn.
- **Parallel subagent calls in one message** ‚Äî ad-hoc independent investigations: "explore X while I check Y", multi-file reviews, independent edits. One message, N `Agent` tool uses, results gathered in parallel.
- **Dynamic workflow** ‚Äî default for substantive multi-step work; the script holds the loop, branching, and intermediate results. See CLAUDE.md `## Plan Execution & Orchestration`.
- **Named team** ‚Äî long-running peers needing agent-to-agent handoffs mid-run, via `TeamCreate`. Sized for a handful of peers; a teammate's own subagents are foreground-only, so an N-unit sweep inside a team delegates to a workflow instead of nesting `Agent` calls.

Single-step exception: one task, no parallel sibling, no follow-on ‚Üí one subagent call is fine.

## Writing Plans

When you write a plan ‚Äî in plan mode, or any "here's what I'll do" before you start editing ‚Äî use this shape so it's fast to scan and complete enough to execute:

- **Context** ‚Äî why this change: the problem or need, what prompted it, the intended outcome.
- **Approach** ‚Äî the recommended approach only (not every alternative you weighed), as ordered steps. Name the critical files to touch; for a pattern repeated across many files, describe it once with a few representative paths instead of listing them all. Cite existing utilities/patterns you'll reuse, with their paths.
- **Potential Pitfalls** ‚Äî the sharp edges specific to this work: ordering constraints, code that looks safe to change but isn't, prior art that must not be "fixed", state that diverges from how it's described. One bullet each ‚Äî front-load the gotchas you'd otherwise hit mid-implementation.
- **Workflow Plan** ‚Äî required in every plan; a plan without it is incomplete. One line on what the main agent alone does (track state, dispatch, decide, report), then a `Phase | Shape | Agents | Blocks on | Verification` table covering every fan-out the plan anticipates: Shape is `pipeline` / `parallel` / `loop`; Agents names each phase's model and effort per the Models table (e.g. `opus xhigh √ó4`, `gpt-5.6-sol via codex-wrapper √ó2`, `sonnet low ‚Üí codex`); Blocks on names the upstream phase(s) whose output this phase consumes, `‚Äî` when it consumes none; Verification names the check that gates each phase's output. The table is the schedule's dependency graph, and the plan commits to running it at full width: every `‚Äî` row dispatches together at t=0, phases without an edge between them run concurrently, and an edge exists only where a named artifact or verdict is consumed ‚Äî a plan that sequences phases on anything vaguer is incomplete. Speculative lanes are rows of their own ‚Äî marked `(speculative)`, `Blocks on ‚Äî`, worktree-isolated, the verdict that lands or discards them named in their Verification cell (CLAUDE.md ¬ß Plan Execution & Orchestration). When nothing fans out, one line saying everything stays at the main-agent level replaces the table.
- **Verification** ‚Äî how to prove it works end to end: the exact commands to run, tests to add, and behavior to observe.

## Compact Context (ccx)

`cc-context` ‚Äî the `ccx` CLI and the `cc-context` MCP (its tools mirror the query surface ‚Äî read, search, symbol, outline, diff, edit ‚Äî plus `ccx_exec`/`ccx_exec_tools` for multi-call composition and `BashFormat` for JSON re-encoding) ‚Äî is the first stop for TARGETED code questions: a file section, a symbol, a search, a diff. It returns token-bounded output (signatures + line numbers, explicit overflow, never silent truncation) instead of raw dumps, and the capt-hook `ccx` guard pack rewrites the mappable token-heavy commands (raw `grep`, bare `git diff`/`git show`, page-dump `curl`, oversized `Read`s) to their ccx equivalents in place and BLOCKS the rest. That budget discipline covers ccx itself: never re-truncate ccx output with `| head`/`| tail` ‚Äî it re-introduces silent truncation and eats the overflow footer; raise `--budget`, narrow with `--section`/`--scope`, or compose with `ccx exec` (entry 16).

1. **Orient a repo** ‚Üí `ccx repo overview`
2. **"How does X work / where is Y" (intent)** ‚Üí `ccx code search "<question>"` (semantic ‚Äî a native in-process engine, no external process; model weights download once into a local cache on first use; `--content` narrows the corpus, space-separated `code|docs|config|all` defaulting to `code docs`, honored by `ccx code search`/`related` and the MCP search/related tools)
3. **A specific symbol (where + signature)** ‚Üí `ccx code symbol <name>` (alias `ccx code grok`; terse by default with a counts trailer ‚Äî `--callers`/`--callees`/`--body`/`--full` expand, `--budget N` caps an expanded card)
4. **Literal or regex text** ‚Üí `ccx code grep <text> [paths...] [--regex] [--glob G] [--scope dir] [-i] [-w]` (literal-first with auto-regex: a zero-match literal pass whose pattern carries regex metachars and compiles reruns as a regex, the header saying `(auto-regex)` ‚Äî so `a|b` works without the flag; `--regex` forces regex from the start; `--regex`/`-i`/`-w` and explicit file operands run on ripgrep; `--glob` filters within explicit paths; system `grep` fills in when `rg` is missing; a glob or scope anchored at a real path ‚Äî `.venv/‚Ä¶/pkg/*.py` ‚Äî is searched even where ignore rules would hide it)
5. **List files** ‚Üí `ccx repo find "<glob>" [--scope dir] [--budget N]` (gitignore-honoring, VCS stores skipped, sorted; budget-capped with a footer counting withheld rows and ignore-hidden files; a glob anchored at a real path ‚Äî `.venv/**/*.py` ‚Äî lists files ignore rules would hide; orientation is entry 1, not `"**/*"`)
6. **Read a file** ‚Üí `ccx code outline <file-or-dir>` first (ast-grep structural map for the languages it outlines and any directory, a markdown heading outline or an honest head window otherwise; top-level by default ‚Äî `--deep` expands members, `--section A-B` windows a file), then `ccx code read <file> --section A-B` for the part you need (whole file: `ccx code read <file> --full`)
7. **Edit a file** ‚Üí `ccx code edit <file> --at A-B#hash --content <text>` (hash-verified write: refuses on anchor mismatch, re-anchors moved content, returns the new anchor so edits chain; `--content -` reads stdin, `--delete` removes the range; `--match <text>` addresses by exact bytes instead of a span ‚Äî an ambiguous match errors listing candidate anchors, `--at` scopes the scan, `--all` replaces every occurrence)
8. **Review changes** ‚Üí `ccx vcs diff [src]` (structural, jj-aware; exact hunks: `git diff -- <file>`)
9. **Inspect one commit** ‚Üí `ccx vcs show [ref]` (message + structural per-file diff; default `@-`/HEAD)
10. **How a file evolved** ‚Üí `ccx vcs history <path> [-n N]` (per-commit sha ¬∑ date ¬∑ subject + changed symbols)
11. **Locate a repo/module/package on disk** ‚Üí `ccx repo locate <name>` (sibling repo, Go module, or Python package by import or dist name; prints tab-separated `kind`/`path`/`version` rows ‚Äî an installed Python package yields both its sibling `repo` row and its installed `package` row; exit 3 when nothing resolves)
12. **Read installed dependency source** (`.venv`, site-packages, vendored) ‚Üí spawn the `cc-context:dep-reader` agent with the package and your question ‚Äî cited conclusions come back, the source never enters your context. Inline: `ccx repo locate <pkg>` for the on-disk path, then entries 4/6 with that path (`--scope <path>` or an anchored glob) ‚Äî never raw `rg` into `.venv`
13. **Commit, push, watch CI** ‚Üí `ccx vcs ship -m "<msg>" [paths...]` (jj-aware commit + push + a watch over every workflow run on the pushed commit, in one call ‚Äî per-run `workflow ¬∑ conclusion ¬∑ duration ¬∑ url` report, plus failing jobs and a `--budget`-capped log excerpt when a run goes red; trailing paths scope the commit to just those files, leaving the rest of a shared working copy untouched; scoping goes below the file too ‚Äî `ccx vcs hunks [paths...]` lists every pending hunk as a stable `file:A-B#hash` ref, and repeatable `--skip-hunk <ref>`/`--only-hunk <ref>` commit a file partially without ever rewriting it on disk, leaving excluded hunks (a concurrent session's, say) uncommitted in `@`, and refusing outright when a selection would cut an empty commit or a ref has drifted; an empty working copy refuses outright too (`nothing to commit` ‚Äî a prior ship likely already landed it) instead of cutting an empty duplicate; and the push only auto-advances the trunk bookmark ‚Äî advancing any other needs an explicit `--bookmark <name>`, resolved before the commit forms so a refusal leaves the working copy untouched; an updated remote is handled in-call on both backends ‚Äî ship fetches first and rebases the stack onto the target (jj bookmark, or `origin/<branch>` on plain git, autostashing uncommitted work), rolling back and reporting a rebase that would conflict, and a push that still loses the race to a concurrent push re-fetches, re-rebases, and retries up to 3 attempts before failing with the manual recovery steps ‚Äî though a rejected `--amend` push refuses instead of force-retrying over the other session's work). A live Graphite config (`.git/.graphite_repo_config`) routes ship to the gt lane instead ‚Äî even in colocated jj repos; `--no-gt` falls back to the jj>git detection: staging and commits go through gt (`gt add`, then on trunk `-m` auto-creates a stacked branch named from the message; on a stacked branch `-m` appends a commit and `--amend` amends it; an untracked branch is adopted first with `gt track -f`; `--create` stacks a new branch on top ‚Äî an explicit name is spelled `--create=name`, since a bare `--create name` parses `name` as a path), and the push becomes `gt submit` of the downstack ‚Äî published by default, `--draft` opts out, `--publish` says so explicitly ‚Äî with no fetch/rebase/retry, since gt owns restacking: ship refuses up front on a `needs_restack` anywhere on the downstack (`gt restack`, then re-run), on a branch gt cannot adopt, or on `--amend` on trunk, and a failed submit reports gt's recovery step (`gt restack`/`gt sync`/`gt auth`) instead of retrying, the report reading `submitted <branch> ‚Üí PR #<n> <url>`. `--reviews` chains a review watch (entry 14) after the CI watch. A red run hands off to the `cc-context:ci-triage` agent ‚Äî spawn it with the run id for root cause, a minimal excerpt, and the next step instead of the full log
14. **Stream PR review comments** ‚Üí `ccx vcs reviews [pr-or-branch...]` (attaches to open PRs ‚Äî positionals mix PR numbers and branch names, none ‚Üí the current branch's PR, `--stack` ‚Üí the Graphite downstack ‚Äî and polls every `--interval` (default 30s), streaming each review verdict, inline comment, and issue comment as one block with author, locus, and a `--budget`-capped body; a `changes_requested` review carries a pointer to spawn the `cc-context:pr-review-triage` agent with the PR and review id; the watch exits when every watched PR is merged or closed, and Ctrl-C prints the exact resume command; `--since` replays history ‚Äî RFC3339, a duration ago like `90m`, or `all`, default now ‚Äî so arm a `Monitor` on `ccx vcs reviews --since 5m` right after a ship to close the gap between push and attach; the watch is at-least-once, so a resume may re-print events already seen; `ship --reviews` chains the same watch in-call after CI)
15. **Bring the stack current with trunk** ‚Üí `ccx vcs restack` (alias `rebase`; lane-selected like ship ‚Äî a live Graphite config runs `gt sync`, jj fetches and rebases the working-copy stack onto `trunk()`, plain git fetches and rebases onto the remote default branch with `--autostash`; a conflict always refuses with the lane's recovery steps ‚Äî `gt continue`/`gt abort`, a rolled-back jj rebase, an aborted git rebase ‚Äî never headless resolution; `--no-gt` forces the jj/git lane)
16. **Compose several calls / post-process any output** ‚Üí `ccx exec '<python>'` ‚Äî a sandboxed script whose async host functions are every ccx query op, a gated `sh(cmd)`, and every stateless MCP server's tools (auto-reflected, no flag needed); only the script's return value enters context. Rule of thumb: one question ‚Üí one ccx call (entries 1‚Äì15, 17‚Äì20); a pipeline, filter, fan-out, or any output you'd immediately post-process (project a JSON blob, sweep signatures across files, join search hits) ‚Üí exec. Discover the host functions and the Python-subset rules with `ccx exec --list-tools` (MCP: `ccx_exec_tools`), once per session.
17. **Re-encode JSON tool output** ‚Üí `ccx format -- <cmd>` (or `‚Ä¶ | ccx format`) ‚Äî a shape classifier picks the leanest encoding (prose, markdown table, CSV/TSV, TOON, TRON, JSONL, or compact JSON), never larger than compact JSON by bytes; `--format=X` forces one encoder
18. **Map a web page** ‚Üí `ccx web outline <url>` (heading tree with stable `¬ß` section refs; pages cache 24h, `--refresh` refetches)
19. **Read one section of a page** ‚Üí `ccx web read <url> --section <ref>` (budget-capped section subtree + prev/next nav; whole page: `--full`)
20. **Ask a question of a page** ‚Üí `ccx web search <url> "<question>"` (top-k relevant chunks with `<url> ¬ß2.3#hash` cites; hybrid BM25 + local embeddings ‚Äî native in-process, no `uv`; BM25-only with a note when the model weights are unavailable, i.e. offline with an empty cache)

Entries 9‚Äì15 are CLI-only (entry 12's `locate` step included) ‚Äî the MCP mirrors the query surface (1‚Äì8) plus exec (16), format (17, as `BashFormat`), and web (18‚Äì20, as `ccx_web_outline`/`ccx_web_read`/`ccx_web_search`), not these.

A path operand missing its extension resolves itself across the path-taking ops: `pkg/events` where only `pkg/events.py` exists runs against the sibling and notes the substitution (`# note: pkg/events ‚Üí pkg/events.py`); several candidates error listing them, none errors clean (exit 3) ‚Äî never raw engine stderr.

A one-shot question about a page ‚Üí spawn the `cc-context:web-fetch` agent with the URL and your prompt verbatim (the WebFetch drop-in: cited answer out, the page never enters your context); research spanning several pages ‚Üí `cc-context:web-researcher`.

Durable prose ‚Äî plans, reviews, memory files ‚Äî cites code as `path:line#hash` (e.g. `internal/render/finalize.go:31#k2fa`); any later session resolves the cite statelessly with ccx, because the hash re-anchors by content even after the file drifts.

Reach for your **LSP** when the answer must be exhaustive/structural (findReferences, rename, goToImplementation) ‚Äî and verify any complete-set answer ("every subclass", "every importer") by reading the candidate files: bounded views optimize for precision, not exhaustiveness ‚Äî or spawn the `cc-context:enumerator` agent, which packages that gather-and-verify loop and returns the verified set with cites and named blind spots. Use **Grep/Glob** or `rg` only for literal content in non-source files (logs, JSON, YAML) ‚Äî on source, raw `rg` is gated the same as raw `grep`.

## Style

**Comments are terse and used sparingly.** Exported symbols and packages receive
godoc that starts with the identifier. Body comments are only for TODOs,
non-obvious workarounds, and load-bearing invariants.

@STYLEGUIDE.md

## General Rules

**No compatibility code.** Old protocol readers, feature negotiation, state
migrations, aliases for deleted APIs, and silent fallbacks are bugs. Consumers
migrate manually at the hard cut.

**Keep ownership exact.** daemonkit owns lifecycle, listeners, transport,
process identity, reaping, trust primitives, and App Group resolution. FuseKit
owns object identity, catalog revisions, tenant lanes, materialization,
presentations, and convergence. Product policy stays in consumers.

**Search before writing.** Query with `ccx code search` or `ccx code symbol`
before creating a helper. Reuse current catalog, tenant, and daemonkit seams.

**Observe, don't infer.** Inspect fixtures, runtime state, and exact protocol
bytes before reasoning. Reproduce the smallest failure before editing.

**Verify before asserting.** Do not report success until the relevant test,
build, runtime probe, or residue scan has completed.

**Testing.** Always use `scripts/test.sh`. The per-commit gate is
`scripts/test.sh -race -count=1 ./...`, `go vet ./...`, and
`CGO_ENABLED=0 go build ./...`. Protocol changes run every generator in check
mode, `swift build`, and `swift test`. Fuse-tag compilation needs FUSE-T or
libfuse; live mount, kill/reap, File Provider, and TCC tests run only in isolated
VMs.

**Writing docs.** Keep examples on the current hard API only. Do not document
removed behavior as an alternative or migration path.

**Version control.** This repo is a colocated `jj` repo over git ‚Äî prefer `jj` (`jj describe` / `jj commit`, `jj git push`) over raw `git` for day-to-day work. Commits stay atomic and scoped: one logical change each. For the routine commit, push, and watch-CI cycle, `ccx vcs ship -m "<msg>"` runs the whole dance in one call ‚Äî a jj-aware commit, the push, and a watch over every workflow run on the pushed commit, with a per-run report and failure logs ‚Äî instead of the three-to-six Bash calls by hand. A working copy shared with a concurrent session is no reason to bypass ship: `ccx vcs ship -m "<msg>" <paths...>` commits only your paths and leaves the rest of `@` untouched. A single file mixing your hunks with a concurrent session's is no reason either: `ccx vcs hunks <file>` lists each pending hunk as a `file:A-B#hash` ref, and `ccx vcs ship --skip-hunk <ref>` commits around the foreign one ‚Äî the partial commit is cut through jj's diff-editor protocol (git: a temp index), never by rewriting the file on disk. Ship also runs the repo's prek commit hooks (`uvx prek`) before committing. Auto-fixes are re-verified and folded into the commit, a hook that still fails after the retry aborts the ship with nothing committed, and `--no-verify` skips the gate. Hunk-scoped ships skip it too and report a `hooks hunk-skip` segment, since a partial selection can't be verified externally. The push only auto-advances the trunk bookmark ‚Äî parked on someone else's bookmark, ship refuses, and `--bookmark <name>` advances a non-trunk bookmark deliberately; target resolution runs before the commit forms, so a refusal leaves the working copy exactly as it was. Ship also refuses an empty working copy (`nothing to commit ‚Äî did a prior ship already land <sha>?`), so a double-run cuts no empty duplicate ‚Äî a no-diff merge or a conflicted `@` still commits. Drop to the manual `jj` steps when ship still doesn't fit, like a multi-commit split. An updated remote is not one of those cases: ship fetches first and rebases the stack onto the target itself ‚Äî on plain-git repos too, autostashing uncommitted work ‚Äî rolling back and reporting a rebase that would conflict, and a push rejected because origin advanced again mid-ship re-fetches, re-rebases, and retries (up to 3 attempts) before failing with the manual steps. A rejected `--amend` push is the exception: ship refuses rather than force-retrying over the concurrent push. The manual recovery ‚Äî `jj git fetch` then `jj rebase` (your in-flight `@` rides along untouched) ‚Äî remains for non-ship flows and for resolving a conflicted rebase ship refused; never `git stash` or a worktree + cherry-pick dance.

**Watch CI after every push.** A push that kicks off CI isn't done until every run is green. `ccx vcs ship` folds this in ‚Äî it pushes, then watches every workflow run on the pushed commit (found by `--commit`, retrying registration for up to a minute) and reports each run's conclusion, duration, and URL, plus failing jobs and a `--budget`-capped log excerpt when a run goes red; on a terminal the watch streams live. A `CI error` segment means the watch itself hit an infra failure after a successful push ‚Äî the report's `check:` line says how to resume; a bare re-run of ship refuses (nothing to commit) instead of cutting a duplicate. For a push ship didn't make, watch the run to completion yourself before you stop ‚Äî `gh run watch "$(gh run list -L1 --json databaseId -q '.[0].databaseId')" --exit-status` ‚Äî and never walk away from a red run: when the failures are mechanical (lint, format, vet ‚Äî pre-existing baseline debt included), fix them forward in an immediate follow-up commit; report-only (a cc-notes bug) is reserved for failures that need a design or owner decision. (`--exit-status` exits non-zero when the run fails; give the run a moment to register before watching.)

In a repo with a live Graphite config (`.git/.graphite_repo_config`), ship routes to the gt lane: it cuts a stacked branch via `gt create`/`gt modify` (adopting an untracked branch with `gt track -f` first) and lands it as a submitted PR ‚Äî published by default, `--draft` opts out ‚Äî instead of pushing trunk; `--no-gt` restores the jj/git flow. `ccx vcs restack` (alias `rebase`) is the one-command catch-up with trunk ‚Äî `gt sync` / jj fetch + rebase onto `trunk()` / git fetch + rebase `--autostash` ‚Äî refusing with recovery steps on conflict; and the signal needn't stop at CI: `ship --reviews` keeps streaming review comments until the submitted PRs merge or close, and `ccx vcs reviews` attaches the same watch to an existing PR.
